reasoning: The Biden-era "AI Diffusion Rule," which originally instituted comprehensive controls on model weights and certain AI chips in January 2025, was officially rescinded by the Department of Commerce on May 12, 2025. In its place, the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) issued "guidance" primarily related to existing restrictions on specific Chinese-made AI chips (Huawei's Ascend series) and restricted their use globally, while also warning against using US chips to train Chinese AI models. Because the resolution criteria explicitly state that "export controls must apply broadly, and must not be targeted or limited to a single country or entity," the May directives aimed heavily at China and Huawei, in addition to largely being "guidance" enforcing existing General Prohibitions (GP 10), are unlikely to qualify as "broad application of new or expanded controls." While BIS indicated they will issue replacement rules, as of the knowledge cutoff (November 2025), no new broad comprehensive rules on AI chips, cloud access, or model weights have been finalized with an effective date in 2025 that meet the required "broad application" without singling out a specific country/entity. The probability is therefore kept relatively low, as the primary 2025 action ended up being a rescission of regulations rather than a broad expansion.
